questions.question: Two signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are orthogonal if

explanations.b: Two signals are orthogonal if their inner product is zero: $\int_{T_1}^{T_2} x_1(t)x_2(t)dt = 0$. This is equivalent to their dot product being zero over the interval. Simply having zero product at any point or zero sum is not sufficient for orthogonality.

questions.question: The signals $\sin (t)$ and $\sin (\alpha t)$ are orthogonal in the interval $[0, 2\pi]$ if

answers.c: $\alpha$ is any integer other than 1 or -1

explanations.c: For sinusoidal functions to be orthogonal in $[0,2\pi]$, we need $\int_0^{2\pi} \sin(t)\sin(\alpha t)dt = 0$. This is true for all integer values of $\alpha$ except when $\alpha = \pm 1$, as these cases result in non-zero integrals.

questions.question: Which of the signals are orthogonal to each other, $x_1(t)=\psi(t), x_2(t)=\cos (2 \pi t), x_3(t)=\psi(2 t)$ in the period $[0,1]$?

explanations.d: Both $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are orthogonal because $\int_0^1 \psi(t)\cos(2\pi t)dt = 0$, and $x_1(t)$ and $x_3(t)$ are orthogonal because $\int_0^1 \psi(t)\psi(2t)dt = 0$ due to their different frequency components.

questions.question: Which pairs of signals are orthogonal among $x_1(t)=\cos (t), x_2(t)=5 \sin (5 t), x_3(t)=\sin (t)$ in the period $[0, 2\pi]$?

explanations.d: Both $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are orthogonal because $\int_0^1 \psi(t)\cos(2\pi t)dt = 0$, and $x_1(t)$ and $x_3(t)$ are orthogonal because $\int_0^1 \psi(t)\psi(2t)dt = 0$ due to their different frequency components.

questions.question: If vectors $v_1$ and $v_2$ are orthogonal and vectors $v_2$ and $v_3$ are orthogonal, then which of the following statement is true

explanations.b: While $v_1 \perp v_2$ and $v_2 \perp v_3$, orthogonality is not a transitive property. Consider vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$: $v_1=[1,0,0]$, $v_2=[0,1,0]$, and $v_3=[1,0,1]$. Here $v_1 \perp v_2$ and $v_2 \perp v_3$, but $v_1 \cdot v_3 \neq 0$.
